Chapter 4: Quantitative Causal Models

Inference and Intervention | BK Advisors

BK Advisors

2026-02-12

Learning Objectives

After this chapter, you will be able to:

  • Define probability distributions over discrete variable states and apply the basic axioms
  • Compute conditional probabilities and construct Conditional Probability Tables (CPTs)
  • Apply Bayes’ rule to update beliefs when new evidence arrives
  • Explain the Causal Markov Condition and how a DAG factorizes a joint distribution
  • Build a quantitative Bayesian network in R using bnlearn and query it for MNH policy decisions

From Qualitative to Quantitative

Structure alone is not enough – we need numbers

Why Structure Alone Is Not Enough

In Chapters 2 and 3, we built qualitative causal models – DAGs with nodes, arrows, and signs.

What qualitative models tell us:

  • Which variables influence which
  • The direction of causal effects ([+] or [-])
  • Which paths are open or blocked (d-separation)

What qualitative models cannot tell us:

  • How much does Staffing affect Quality of Care?
  • How likely is high NMR given equipment shortages?
  • Which intervention has the largest expected impact?

To make decisions, we need to quantify uncertainty.

Quantitative causal models attach probability distributions to every node in the DAG.

The Quantitative Upgrade

Qualitative DAG
(Ch 2-3)
+
Probability
Tables
=
Bayesian
Network

MNH Example

Qualitative: Staffing –> Quality of Care –> Neonatal Mortality Rate

Quantitative: If P(Staffing = Adequate) = 0.40, and P(Quality = Good | Staffing = Adequate) = 0.80, then what is P(NMR = High)?

Now we can compute the answer, not just reason about direction.

A Bayesian network = a DAG + a conditional probability table (CPT) for every node. This chapter teaches you how to build and query one.

Probability Fundamentals

The language of quantitative uncertainty

Variable States

Every variable in our causal model takes on a set of states (values).

States must be discrete and MECE (Mutually Exclusive, Collectively Exhaustive) – every observation falls into exactly one state.

Variable States MECE?
Staffing {Adequate, Low} Yes – every facility is one or the other
CPAP Available {Available, Unavailable} Yes
Quality of Care {Good, Poor} Yes
NMR {High (>25/1000), Low ($$25/1000)} Yes

Why discrete? Continuous variables (exact NMR = 27.3/1000) require density functions. We discretize for tractability – a common and effective practice in applied Bayesian networks.

Events: Sets of States

An event is any subset of states for one or more variables. Events can be combined using set operations.

Simple events:

  • \(A\) = {Staffing = Adequate}
  • \(B\) = {CPAP = Available}
  • \(C\) = {NMR = High}

Compound events:

  • \(A \cap B\) = Adequate staffing AND CPAP available
  • \(A \cup C\) = Adequate staffing OR high NMR
  • \(\bar{A}\) = Staffing is NOT adequate (i.e., Low)

For binary variables, knowing one state determines the other: if P(Staffing = Adequate) = 0.40, then P(Staffing = Low) = 1 - 0.40 = 0.60.

Probability Axioms

The three axioms that all probabilities must satisfy:

Axiom 1 (Non-negativity): \(P(A) \geq 0\) for any event \(A\)

Axiom 2 (Normalization): \(P(\Omega) = 1\) where \(\Omega\) is the entire sample space

Axiom 3 (Additivity): If \(A \cap B = \emptyset\), then \(P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)\)

Immediate consequences:

  • \(P(\bar{A}) = 1 - P(A)\) (complement rule)
  • \(0 \leq P(A) \leq 1\) (probabilities are bounded)
  • For a variable with states \(\{s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_k\}\): \(\sum_{i=1}^{k} P(s_i) = 1\)

Frequency vs. Subjective Probability

Frequentist interpretation:

Probability = long-run relative frequency.

“40% of facilities have adequate staffing” means: if we visit many facilities, about 40% will be adequately staffed.

Based on data (e.g., facility surveys).

Subjective (Bayesian) interpretation:

Probability = degree of belief.

“I believe there’s a 70% chance this district will reduce NMR by 2030” – an expert judgment.

Based on evidence + reasoning.

In practice, we use both. CPTs combine survey data where available with expert judgment where data is sparse. The Beginnings Fund operates in data-limited settings – subjective priors are unavoidable and valuable.

MNH Example: Marginal vs. Conditional Probability

Consider neonatal mortality and preterm birth in sub-Saharan Africa:

Marginal probability: \(P(\text{Neonatal Death}) \approx 0.025\) (25 per 1,000 live births)

Conditional probability: \(P(\text{Neonatal Death} \mid \text{Preterm}) \approx 0.10\) (100 per 1,000 preterm births)

Key Insight

The probability of neonatal death is 4 times higher for preterm births than for the general population. This tells us that preterm birth is a strong risk factor – but is it a cause?

To answer that, we need a causal model that separates the direct effect of prematurity from confounders like maternal health, facility quality, and access to care.

Conditional Probability

P(A|B): The foundation of Bayesian networks

Definition and Interpretation

Conditional Probability: The probability of event \(A\) given that event \(B\) has occurred (or is known to be true):

\[P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}, \quad P(B) > 0\]

Intuition

\(P(A \mid B)\) answers: “Among the cases where \(B\) is true, what fraction also have \(A\)?”

\(P(\text{NMR=High} \mid \text{Staffing=Low})\) = “Among facilities with low staffing, what fraction have high neonatal mortality?”

Crucial distinction:

  • \(P(\text{NMR=High} \mid \text{Staffing=Low}) = 0.55\) – 55% of under-staffed facilities have high NMR
  • \(P(\text{Staffing=Low} \mid \text{NMR=High}) = 0.75\) – 75% of high-NMR facilities are under-staffed

These are not the same – confusing them is a common and costly error.

Stochastic Independence

Two events \(A\) and \(B\) are stochastically independent if and only if:

\[P(A \mid B) = P(A)\]

Equivalently: \(P(A \cap B) = P(A) \times P(B)\)

What independence means: Knowing \(B\) tells you nothing about \(A\).

MNH Test

Is Staffing independent of NMR? We check:

  • \(P(\text{NMR=High}) = 0.35\) (overall)
  • \(P(\text{NMR=High} \mid \text{Staffing=Adequate}) = 0.15\)
  • \(P(\text{NMR=High} \mid \text{Staffing=Low}) = 0.55\)

Since \(0.15 \neq 0.35\) and \(0.55 \neq 0.35\), Staffing and NMR are not independent. Staffing is informative about mortality.

Conditional Probability Tables (CPTs)

A Conditional Probability Table (CPT) lists \(P(\text{Child} \mid \text{Parents})\) for every combination of parent and child states. Every row sums to 1.

CPT for Quality of Care given Staffing and CPAP:

Staffing CPAP Available P(Quality=Good) P(Quality=Poor)
Adequate Available 0.85 0.15
Adequate Unavailable 0.55 0.45
Low Available 0.50 0.50
Low Unavailable 0.20 0.80

Each row defines a conditional distribution over Quality of Care. The table encodes four scenarios – the core of what the Bayesian network “knows.”

MNH CPT: Neonatal Outcome Given CPAP and Training

A CPT for neonatal outcome given equipment and workforce readiness:

CPAP Available Staff Trained P(Outcome=Survival) P(Outcome=Death)
Available Completed 0.96 0.04
Available Not Completed 0.88 0.12
Unavailable Completed 0.82 0.18
Unavailable Not Completed 0.70 0.30

Reading the table: When both CPAP is available and staff are trained, neonatal survival is 96%. When neither is present, survival drops to 70% – a 26 percentage point gap. This quantifies the combined effect of equipment and training.

Note: These probabilities are illustrative but grounded in the literature on neonatal care in low-resource settings. In practice, CPTs are estimated from facility-level data, expert elicitation, or both.

Joint Probabilities

P(X, Y): The full picture of how variables co-occur

The Factoring Rule

The joint probability of two events is computed by the product (chain) rule:

\[P(X, Y) = P(X \mid Y) \times P(Y)\]

This generalizes to any number of variables:

\[P(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(X_i \mid X_1, \ldots, X_{i-1})\]

Simple Example

\(P(\text{Staffing=Low, Quality=Poor})\)

\(= P(\text{Quality=Poor} \mid \text{Staffing=Low}) \times P(\text{Staffing=Low})\)

\(= 0.65 \times 0.60 = 0.39\)

About 39% of facilities are both under-staffed and deliver poor quality care.

Joint Probability Tables

A joint probability table shows \(P(X, Y)\) for every combination of states:

Staffing = Adequate Staffing = Low Row Total
Quality = Good 0.28 0.21 0.49
Quality = Poor 0.12 0.39 0.51
Column Total 0.40 0.60 1.00
  • Cell values = joint probabilities: P(Quality=Good, Staffing=Adequate) = 0.28
  • Row totals = marginal probabilities of Quality: P(Quality=Good) = 0.49
  • Column totals = marginal probabilities of Staffing: P(Staffing=Adequate) = 0.40
  • Grand total must equal 1.00 (normalization)

Computing Joint Distributions from CPTs

Given a Bayesian network’s CPTs, we can compute any joint distribution:

1
Start with marginals
P(Staffing)
–>
2
Apply CPTs
P(Quality | Staffing)
–>
3
Multiply
P(Staffing, Quality)

\[P(\text{Staffing=Adequate, Quality=Good}) = P(\text{Quality=Good} \mid \text{Staffing=Adequate}) \times P(\text{Staffing=Adequate})\] \[= 0.70 \times 0.40 = 0.28\]

This “forward” computation – multiplying marginals by CPTs – is the engine of Bayesian network inference. Software automates it, but understanding the mechanics is essential.

MNH Example: Three-Variable Joint Distribution

Compute the joint distribution across Training, Competency, and Outcome:

Given:

  • \(P(\text{Training=Completed}) = 0.45\), \(P(\text{Training=Not}) = 0.55\)
  • CPT for Competency | Training and CPT for Outcome | Competency
Training Competency P(Outcome=Survival) Joint P
Completed High (P=0.80) 0.96 \(0.45 \times 0.80 \times 0.96 = 0.346\)
Completed Low (P=0.20) 0.78 \(0.45 \times 0.20 \times 0.78 = 0.070\)
Not High (P=0.30) 0.96 \(0.55 \times 0.30 \times 0.96 = 0.158\)
Not Low (P=0.70) 0.78 \(0.55 \times 0.70 \times 0.78 = 0.300\)

Overall P(Survival) = 0.346 + 0.070 + 0.158 + 0.300 = 0.874 (87.4%)

If training coverage increased from 45% to 80%, P(Survival) rises to 0.914 – an additional 40 lives saved per 1,000 births.

Bayes’ Rule

Reversing the arrow: from evidence to cause

Derivation from Conditional Probability

Bayes’ rule follows directly from the definition of conditional probability:

We know: \(P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}\) and \(P(B \mid A) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(A)}\)

Both have \(P(A \cap B)\) in the numerator, so:

\[\boxed{P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \times P(A)}{P(B)}}\]

What Bayes’ rule does: It lets us “flip” the conditioning. If we know \(P(\text{Evidence} \mid \text{Cause})\), Bayes’ rule gives us \(P(\text{Cause} \mid \text{Evidence})\).

This is the fundamental operation of situational assessment – reasoning backward from what we observe to what is likely true.

The Terminology of Bayes’ Rule

\[P(\underbrace{A}_{\text{hypothesis}} \mid \underbrace{B}_{\text{evidence}}) = \frac{\overbrace{P(B \mid A)}^{\text{likelihood}} \times \overbrace{P(A)}^{\text{prior}}}{\underbrace{P(B)}_{\text{marginal likelihood}}}\]

Term Symbol Meaning
Prior \(P(A)\) What we believed before seeing evidence
Likelihood \(P(B \mid A)\) How probable the evidence is if the hypothesis is true
Posterior \(P(A \mid B)\) Updated belief after incorporating evidence
Marginal likelihood \(P(B)\) Total probability of the evidence across all hypotheses

Posterior \(\propto\) Likelihood \(\times\) Prior

Evidence updates our beliefs. Strong evidence (high likelihood ratio) shifts the posterior far from the prior.

Computing P(B): The Law of Total Probability

The denominator of Bayes’ rule requires marginalizing over all hypotheses:

\[P(B) = \sum_{\text{all } a} P(B \mid A=a) \times P(A=a)\]

Example with binary hypothesis

If \(A\) has states {Yes, No}:

\(P(B) = P(B \mid A=\text{Yes}) \times P(A=\text{Yes}) + P(B \mid A=\text{No}) \times P(A=\text{No})\)

This is a weighted average of the likelihoods, weighted by the priors.

The denominator ensures the posterior sums to 1 across all hypotheses. It acts as a normalizing constant.

MNH Bayes’ Rule: Diagnosing Facility Gaps

Question: A neonatal death has occurred. What is the probability the facility lacked CPAP?

Given:

  • \(P(\text{CPAP=Unavailable}) = 0.65\) (prior: 65% of facilities lack CPAP)
  • \(P(\text{Death} \mid \text{CPAP=Unavailable}) = 0.05\) (5% mortality without CPAP)
  • \(P(\text{Death} \mid \text{CPAP=Available}) = 0.015\) (1.5% mortality with CPAP)

Step 1: Compute \(P(\text{Death})\)

\(P(\text{Death}) = 0.05 \times 0.65 + 0.015 \times 0.35 = 0.0325 + 0.00525 = 0.03775\)

Step 2: Apply Bayes’ rule

\[P(\text{CPAP=Unavailable} \mid \text{Death}) = \frac{0.05 \times 0.65}{0.03775} = \frac{0.0325}{0.03775} = 0.861\]

MNH Bayes’ Rule: Interpreting the Result

\[P(\text{CPAP=Unavailable} \mid \text{Death}) = 0.861\]

Prior: P(No CPAP) = 0.65

Before learning about the death, we estimated a 65% chance the facility lacked CPAP.

Posterior: P(No CPAP | Death) = 0.86

After learning of the death, the probability the facility lacked CPAP rises to 86%.

The evidence shifted our belief by 21 percentage points.

Observing a neonatal death is strong evidence of CPAP absence. This is the logic behind mortality audits – deaths are diagnostic signals that help us identify facility-level gaps.

Policy implication: Mortality audit data can be used to prioritize CPAP distribution – target facilities where deaths are occurring, since Bayes’ rule tells us these are disproportionately the facilities lacking equipment.

Factorization & the Causal Markov Condition

How DAGs simplify probability

DAG Implies Factorization

Causal Markov Condition: Each variable in a DAG is conditionally independent of its non-descendants, given its parents.

This means the full joint distribution factorizes according to the DAG:

\[P(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(X_i \mid \text{Parents}(X_i))\]

Illustration

For the DAG: Staffing –> Quality –> NMR

\(P(\text{Staffing, Quality, NMR}) = P(\text{Staffing}) \times P(\text{Quality} \mid \text{Staffing}) \times P(\text{NMR} \mid \text{Quality})\)

Note: NMR depends on Quality only, not directly on Staffing. The DAG tells us Staffing’s effect on NMR is fully mediated by Quality.

Why Factorization Matters

Without the DAG (brute-force joint distribution):

  • 4 binary variables = \(2^4 = 16\) entries in the joint table
  • 10 binary variables = \(2^{10} = 1{,}024\) entries
  • 20 binary variables = \(2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576\) entries

With the DAG (factorized CPTs):

  • Each node only needs a CPT conditioned on its parents (typically 1–3 variables)
  • 20 binary variables with max 3 parents each = at most \(20 \times 2^3 = 160\) parameters

The DAG reduces complexity exponentially.

Instead of specifying a million probabilities, the causal structure tells us most variables are conditionally independent, letting us specify just a few hundred. This is why causal models are computationally tractable.

Independence from the DAG

The Causal Markov Condition gives us testable independence predictions:

Staffing
–>
Quality
of Care
–>
NMR
Statement True? Why?
Staffing \(\perp\) NMR No Connected via open path through Quality
Staffing \(\perp\) NMR \(\mid\) Quality Yes Conditioning on Quality blocks the only path

Implication: Once we know Quality of Care, learning about Staffing tells us nothing additional about NMR. The DAG encodes this independence, and the CPTs respect it – \(P(\text{NMR} \mid \text{Quality})\) has no Staffing column.

MNH Application: Quantifying the Model

Adding numbers to the MNH causal model

The Quantified MNH DAG

We take the qualitative model from Ch 2 and attach CPTs to each node:

Staffing
P(Adequate)=0.40
Equipment
P(Available)=0.35
| |
Quality of Care
CPT: 4 rows
|
NMR
CPT: 2 rows

Four nodes, three CPTs (root nodes only need marginals). This compact representation encodes the full joint distribution over \(2^4 = 16\) combinations of states.

Full CPTs for the MNH Model

Root nodes (marginal distributions):

Variable P(Adequate/Available) P(Low/Unavailable)
Staffing 0.40 0.60
Equipment 0.35 0.65

Quality of Care | Staffing, Equipment:

Staffing Equipment P(Good) P(Poor)
Adequate Available 0.85 0.15
Adequate Unavailable 0.55 0.45
Low Available 0.50 0.50
Low Unavailable 0.20 0.80

NMR | Quality of Care:

Quality P(NMR=Low) P(NMR=High)
Good 0.85 0.15
Poor 0.40 0.60

Querying the Model: Best vs. Worst Case

Best case: Staffing = Adequate, Equipment = Available

\(P(\text{Quality=Good} \mid \text{Adequate, Available}) = 0.85\)

\(P(\text{NMR=High}) = P(\text{High} \mid \text{Good}) \times 0.85 + P(\text{High} \mid \text{Poor}) \times 0.15\) \(= 0.15 \times 0.85 + 0.60 \times 0.15 = 0.1275 + 0.09 = \mathbf{0.2175}\)

Worst case: Staffing = Low, Equipment = Unavailable

\(P(\text{Quality=Good} \mid \text{Low, Unavailable}) = 0.20\)

\(P(\text{NMR=High}) = 0.15 \times 0.20 + 0.60 \times 0.80 = 0.03 + 0.48 = \mathbf{0.51}\)

P(NMR=High) ranges from 21.8% (best case) to 51.0% (worst case).

The combined effect of adequate staffing and available equipment reduces the probability of high NMR by 29.2 percentage points.

Sensitivity Analysis: Which Parent Matters More?

We isolate each parent’s effect by varying one while holding the other at its less favorable level:

Scenario P(NMR=High) Change vs. Worst Case
Worst: Low Staff, No Equipment 0.510 –
Fix Staffing only: Adequate Staff, No Equipment 0.333 -0.177
Fix Equipment only: Low Staff, Has Equipment 0.375 -0.135
Best: Adequate Staff, Has Equipment 0.218 -0.293

Staffing has the larger marginal effect (-17.7 pp) compared to Equipment (-13.5 pp). In resource-constrained settings, investing in workforce may yield greater returns.

But the effects are synergistic: fixing both produces a 29.3 pp reduction – greater than the sum of the individual effects (17.7 + 13.5 = 31.2 pp, but there is overlap).

Exercise: Compute the Probabilities

Your turn: Using the CPTs from the previous slides, compute:

  1. \(P(\text{Quality=Good})\) – the marginal probability of good quality across all facilities

  2. \(P(\text{NMR=High})\) – the overall probability of high NMR

  3. \(P(\text{Staffing=Low} \mid \text{NMR=High})\) – using Bayes’ rule, the probability that a high-NMR facility has low staffing

Hints:

  • For (1): Marginalize over all 4 combinations of Staffing \(\times\) Equipment
  • For (2): Use your answer to (1) and the NMR|Quality CPT
  • For (3): Apply Bayes’ rule – you’ll need P(NMR=High | Staffing=Low), P(Staffing=Low), and P(NMR=High) from parts (1) and (2)

Answers will be revealed in the R workshop section below!

R Code Workshop

Building and querying a Bayesian network in R

R Block 1: Define the Network Structure

library(bnlearn)

# Define the DAG structure using bnlearn's model string syntax
# [Node] means no parents; [Node|Parent1:Parent2] means conditional on parents
dag <- model2network(
  "[Staffing][Equipment][Quality|Staffing:Equipment][NMR|Quality]"
)

# Inspect the structure
dag
nodes(dag)    # "Staffing" "Equipment" "Quality" "NMR"
arcs(dag)     # Shows the 3 directed edges

Reading the model string:

  • [Staffing] – root node, no parents
  • [Equipment] – root node, no parents
  • [Quality|Staffing:Equipment] – Quality depends on both Staffing and Equipment
  • [NMR|Quality] – NMR depends on Quality only

R Block 2: Specify CPTs

# Define CPTs using arrays (states must match exactly)
cpt_staffing <- array(c(0.40, 0.60), dim = 2,
  dimnames = list(Staffing = c("Adequate", "Low")))

cpt_equipment <- array(c(0.35, 0.65), dim = 2,
  dimnames = list(Equipment = c("Available", "Unavailable")))

cpt_quality <- array(
  c(0.85, 0.15,  # Adequate + Available
    0.50, 0.50,  # Low + Available
    0.55, 0.45,  # Adequate + Unavailable
    0.20, 0.80), # Low + Unavailable
  dim = c(2, 2, 2),
  dimnames = list(
    Quality   = c("Good", "Poor"),
    Staffing  = c("Adequate", "Low"),
    Equipment = c("Available", "Unavailable")))

cpt_nmr <- array(
  c(0.85, 0.15,  # Good quality
    0.40, 0.60), # Poor quality
  dim = c(2, 2),
  dimnames = list(NMR = c("Low", "High"), Quality = c("Good", "Poor")))

# Combine into a fitted BN
bn_mnh <- custom.fit(dag, list(
  Staffing = cpt_staffing, Equipment = cpt_equipment,
  Quality = cpt_quality, NMR = cpt_nmr))

R Block 3: Query the Network

# Query: P(NMR = High | Staffing = Low)
cpquery(bn_mnh,
  event  = (NMR == "High"),
  evidence = (Staffing == "Low"),
  n = 1e6)
# Expected: ~0.44

# Query: P(NMR = High | Staffing = Adequate, Equipment = Available)
cpquery(bn_mnh,
  event  = (NMR == "High"),
  evidence = (Staffing == "Adequate" & Equipment == "Available"),
  n = 1e6)
# Expected: ~0.22

# Exact inference using grain (junction tree)
library(gRain)
bn_grain <- as.grain(bn_mnh)
querygrain(bn_grain, nodes = "NMR")
# Returns marginal P(NMR) across all facilities

Answer to Exercise Q2: querygrain() returns P(NMR=High) \(\approx\) 0.393 – about 39% of facilities have high NMR under current conditions.

R Block 4: Visualize the Network

# Option 1: bnlearn's built-in plot (uses Rgraphviz)
graphviz.plot(bn_mnh, main = "MNH Bayesian Network",
  shape = "rectangle",
  highlight = list(nodes = "NMR", col = "#E24A3F", fill = "#E24A3F"))

# Option 2: ggdag for publication-quality figures
library(ggdag)
library(dagitty)

mnh_dag <- dagitty('dag {
  Staffing [pos="0,0"]
  Equipment [pos="2,0"]
  Quality [pos="1,1"]
  NMR [pos="1,2"]
  Staffing -> Quality
  Equipment -> Quality
  Quality -> NMR
}')

ggdag(mnh_dag) +
  theme_dag() +
  labs(title = "MNH Causal Model (4 Nodes)")

R Block 5: Interventional Queries

# Simulate do(Staffing = Adequate) by mutilating the network
bn_do_adequate <- mutilated(bn_mnh, evidence = list(Staffing = "Adequate"))

# P(NMR | do(Staffing = Adequate))
querygrain(as.grain(bn_do_adequate), nodes = "NMR")

# Compare scenarios
scenarios <- data.frame(
  Scenario = c("Status Quo", "do(Staff=Adequate)",
               "do(Equip=Available)", "do(Both)"),
  P_NMR_High = c(
    querygrain(as.grain(bn_mnh),
               nodes = "NMR")$NMR["High"],
    querygrain(as.grain(
      mutilated(bn_mnh, list(Staffing = "Adequate"))),
               nodes = "NMR")$NMR["High"],
    querygrain(as.grain(
      mutilated(bn_mnh, list(Equipment = "Available"))),
               nodes = "NMR")$NMR["High"],
    querygrain(as.grain(
      mutilated(bn_mnh, list(Staffing = "Adequate",
                              Equipment = "Available"))),
               nodes = "NMR")$NMR["High"]))

# Display comparison
scenarios$Reduction <- scenarios$P_NMR_High[1] - scenarios$P_NMR_High
print(scenarios)

This is the payoff of quantitative causal modeling. The mutilated() function implements the do-operator from Ch 2 – it severs incoming arrows to the intervention variable and sets it to a fixed value, giving us the causal effect rather than a mere correlation.

Key Takeaways

Quantitative causal models = DAG + CPTs = Bayesian Network.

They allow us to compute exact probabilities, update beliefs with Bayes’ rule, and compare interventions using the do-operator.

  • Probability fundamentals: States must be MECE; probabilities must sum to 1
  • Conditional probability tables encode the quantitative relationships in the DAG
  • Bayes’ rule lets us reason backward from evidence to causes – essential for situational assessment
  • The Causal Markov Condition simplifies computation from exponential to manageable
  • R + bnlearn provides a complete toolkit for building, querying, and visualizing Bayesian networks
  • Sensitivity analysis reveals which interventions have the largest expected impact

Looking Ahead

Next: Chapter 5 – Situational Analysis

Now that we can build and query quantitative causal models, we turn to their first major application:

  • Updating beliefs with multiple evidence streams – combining DHIS2 data, facility surveys, and expert judgment
  • Diagnostic inference – given observed outcomes, which upstream causes are most likely?
  • Explaining away – how one piece of evidence changes the relevance of another
  • MNH application – analyzing why NMR remains high in a specific country using real-world data patterns

From Ch 4 to Ch 5: We built the engine (Bayesian network). Now we learn to drive it – using evidence to diagnose problems before prescribing interventions.





Inference and Intervention | Chapter 4: Quantitative Causal Models

BK Advisors
